senses.definition: People or devices that take important information from a larger document and present it in a shorter form, especially in legal or technical work.
examples: The company hired abstractors to prepare short summaries of the contracts.
Modern software abstractors can scan large reports in seconds.
Many title abstractors work closely with real estate lawyers.

senses.definition: Short written summaries of longer texts such as research papers, reports, or legal documents.
examples: The conference book includes abstracts of all accepted papers.
Students should read the abstracts before choosing which articles to study.
The journal requires authors to submit structured abstracts.

senses.definition: Third-person singular of abstract: to take out specific information or a part from something larger, often to use it separately.
examples: The system automatically abstracts key phrases from the text.
The software abstracts data from the raw logs.
The report abstracts only the most relevant statistics.

senses.definition: A substance that draws something out from another substance, especially in old scientific or medical writing.
examples: The early chemist described alcohol as an abstrahent of essential oils.
In the old text, vinegar is treated as an effective abstrahent.
Some abstrahent agents were thought to cleanse the body.

senses.definition: To separate or pull something away from something else, especially in old or technical writing.
examples: The treatise claims that the soul can abstrict itself from the senses.
They attempted to abstrict the pure substance from the mixture.
The process was designed to abstrict impurities from the metal.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of abstrict: separated or withdrawn from something.
examples: His mind seemed completely abstricted from the noisy room.
The abstricted elements were analyzed separately.
In the text, the spirit is abstricted from the body.

senses.definition: Present participle of abstrict: separating or withdrawing something from something else.
examples: The alchemist is abstricting the active principle from the herb.
By abstricting himself from society, he hoped to gain peace.
The method involves abstricting volatile components.

senses.definition: The act of separating or withdrawing something from something else, especially in older or technical usage.
examples: The philosopher describes abstraction as a kind of mental abstriction.
The chemical abstriction of oils was poorly understood at the time.
He wrote about the soul’s abstriction from bodily desires.

senses.definition: Plural of abstriction: several acts or instances of separating or withdrawing things.
examples: The text lists various abstrictions of form from matter.
Different chemical abstrictions were carried out in the lab.
The scholar analyzed the logical abstrictions in the argument.

senses.definition: Third-person singular of abstrict: separates or withdraws something from something else.
examples: The method abstricts the pure oil from the mixture.
The philosopher claims that reason abstricts universal ideas from experience.
The process abstricts harmful substances from the water.

senses.definition: To thrust or push something away or apart, especially in very rare or old use.
examples: The author uses the verb to suggest that the mind abstrudes unwanted thoughts.
The mechanism abstrudes the parts under high pressure.
He tried to abtrude, or rather abstrude, his fears from his mind.

senses.definition: Difficult to understand because it is complicated or deals with very deep ideas.
examples: The professor’s lecture was so abstruse that many students felt lost.
Some legal texts are written in highly abstruse language.
He enjoys reading abstruse works on advanced mathematics.

senses.definition: In a way that is hard to understand because it is very complicated or deep.
examples: The topic was abstrusely explained in the article.
He spoke abstrusely about the nature of reality.
The law is abstrusely worded and confuses many readers.

senses.definition: The quality of being hard to understand because something is very complex or deep.
examples: Students complained about the abstruseness of the textbook.
The abstruseness of his style makes his work difficult to read.
They tried to reduce the abstruseness of the technical report.

senses.definition: Plural of abstruseness: different kinds or instances of being difficult to understand.
examples: The editor noted several abstrusenesses in the manuscript.
Different abstrusenesses arise from the author’s dense style.
They discussed the various abstrusenesses of the theory.

senses.definition: Comparative of abstruse: more difficult to understand.
examples: The second chapter is even abstruser than the first.
As the book continues, the arguments become abstruser.
His later work is abstruser and more technical.

senses.definition: Superlative of abstruse: the most difficult to understand.
examples: This is the abstrusest text I have ever read.
The final chapter presents the abstrusest ideas in the book.
Among his works, this is surely the abstrusest.

senses.definition: The state of being hard to understand; abstruseness, especially in old or rare use.
examples: The abstrusion of his philosophy discouraged many readers.
Critics complained about the needless abstrusion in the treatise.
Such abstrusion does not help clear communication.

senses.definition: Plural of abstrusity: difficult or obscure points or matters.
examples: The teacher patiently explained the abstrusities of the subject.
They joked about the abstrusities of modern philosophy.
Readers may struggle with the abstrusities in this article.

senses.definition: The quality of being very hard to understand; abstruseness.
examples: The abstrusity of the argument makes it inaccessible to most readers.
He was criticized for the abstrusity of his language.
They tried to remove some abstrusity from the text.

senses.definition: To waste away or be gradually destroyed, especially in old or literary use.
examples: His strength seemed to absume day by day.
The ancient building was left to absume in the desert winds.
Time will absume all such monuments.

senses.definition: The act of being used up, destroyed, or wasted away, especially in rare or literary use.
examples: The slow absumption of the city’s walls was recorded in the chronicle.
They feared the total absumption of their resources.
Absorption and absumption are contrasted in the old treatise.

senses.definition: Very silly, unreasonable, or impossible to believe.
examples: It would be absurd to ignore these warning signs.
He made an absurd claim about having never slept.
Those shoes look absurd with that suit.

senses.definition: Relating to the idea that human life has no clear meaning or order and often feels pointless.
examples: The novel explores the absurd nature of existence.
He struggled with the absurd feeling that nothing really mattered.
Absurd questions about meaning kept him awake at night.

senses.definition: A state in which life seems to have no meaning or clear reason.
examples: For many thinkers, the absurd is at the heart of human experience.
He felt trapped in the absurd of modern city life.
The play shows characters struggling with the absurd.

senses.definition: Comparative of absurd: more silly or more unreasonable.
examples: His second excuse was even absurder than the first.
The story grows absurder with every new chapter.
Nothing could be absurder than denying the evidence.

senses.definition: Superlative of absurd: the most silly or most unreasonable.
examples: That is the absurdest thing I’ve ever heard.
Of all his ideas, this is the absurdest.
We laughed at the absurdest parts of the show.

senses.definition: A way of thinking or a style in art and literature that focuses on how life can seem meaningless and unreasonable.
examples: Absurdism is central to the novels of Albert Camus.
The play mixes absurdism with dark humor.
She wrote her thesis on absurdism in twentieth-century drama.

senses.definition: Plural of absurdism: different forms or examples of the idea that life is meaningless and unreasonable, often seen in art or writing.
examples: The collection showcases various absurdisms of the postwar period.
Modern absurdisms often appear in short internet stories.
They discussed the political absurdisms hidden in the play.

senses.definition: Relating to absurdism or showing the belief that life is strange and has no clear meaning.
examples: They staged an absurdist play about office life.
The film has a strongly absurdist tone.
His absurdist comedy hides a serious message.

senses.definition: A person, especially a writer or artist, who follows or creates works based on absurdism.
examples: Beckett is often called an absurdist.
The exhibition brought together several young absurdists.
As an absurdist, she refuses neat endings.

senses.definition: Plural of absurdist: writers, artists, or thinkers who use or support absurdism.
examples: The absurdists of the 1950s changed modern theatre.
Many absurdists question traditional ideas of meaning.
She studied how absurdists use silence on stage.

senses.definition: Things that are very silly, unreasonable, or impossible to believe.
examples: They laughed at the absurdities of the situation.
The book points out the absurdities of modern life.
We must avoid such obvious absurdities in our argument.

senses.definition: The quality of being very silly, unreasonable, or impossible to believe.
examples: He recognized the absurdity of his own plan.
The film shows the absurdity of war.
They laughed at the sheer absurdity of the mistake.

senses.definition: Something that is very silly or unreasonable.
examples: Wearing a coat in this heat is an absurdity.
The rule is an absurdity that should be removed.
Such absurdities have no place in serious debate.

senses.definition: In a way that is very silly, unreasonable, or hard to believe.
examples: The trousers were absurdly expensive.
He behaved absurdly throughout the meeting.
The room was absurdly small for fifty people.

senses.definition: The state of being very silly or unreasonable; absurdity.
examples: We laughed at the absurdness of the whole scene.
The absurdness of the rule soon became clear.
He commented on the absurdness of their demands.

senses.definition: Plural of absurdness: different instances or kinds of being very silly or unreasonable.
examples: They listed the absurdnesses of the new policy.
The novel reveals many absurdnesses in social life.
He laughed at the small absurdnesses of daily routines.

senses.definition: Plural of absurd: things that are very silly or unreasonable, or people who behave in that way.
examples: He dismissed their ideas as mere absurds.
The play is filled with absurds that make the audience think.
They joked that they were a group of happy absurds.

senses.definition: A Latin word used in phrases in logic and mathematics to mean something that is false or impossible.
examples: The proof works by showing that the assumption leads to an absurdum.
They used reductio ad absurdum to complete the argument.
In logic, reaching an absurdum means the original claim cannot be true.

senses.definition: A very small unit of electric potential equal to one billionth of a volt, used in older scientific writing.
examples: The device could detect changes of only a few absvolts.
Early measurements were recorded in absvolts rather than volts.
Such tiny signals, measured in absvolts, are hard to observe.

senses.definition: A name from classical stories and history, sometimes used for a figure in Greek mythology or a person in ancient writings.
examples: The legend of Absyrtus appears in several ancient texts.
In the myth, Absyrtus pursues Jason and Medea.
Scholars debate the role of Absyrtus in the story.

senses.definition: Short form of about, used mainly in informal writing such as messages and notes.
examples: I’ll be there abt 7 pm.
She has abt 20 minutes before the meeting.
They spent abt a week in the mountains.

senses.definition: Situated away from the end or tip of something, especially in medical or biological descriptions.
examples: The abterminal part of the nerve was damaged.
Abterminal regions of the organ showed less activity.
The study focuses on abterminal segments of the tissue.

senses.definition: In old Scottish history, a district or estate held by a thane or local lord.
examples: The king granted him an abthain in the north.
Records mention the ancient abthain near the river.
Control of the abthain brought great influence.

senses.definition: A variant old form referring to the lands or jurisdiction of an abthain in Scotland.
examples: The charter lists the boundaries of the abthainrie.
Disputes over the abthainrie lasted for decades.
He inherited the whole abthainrie from his uncle.

senses.definition: Another historical form for the land or authority belonging to an abthain in Scotland.
examples: Control of the abthainry was highly valued.
The old map marks the borders of the abthainry.
He wrote a study of medieval Scottish abthainries.

senses.definition: Lands or rights belonging to a thane, especially in old Scottish law.
examples: The document describes the extent of the abthanage.
He was accused of taking rents from the royal abthanage.
The crown claimed the abthanage after the family died out.

senses.definition: An old Scottish title for a high-ranking thane or local lord.
examples: The abthane held wide powers over the region.
Legends tell of an abthane who opposed the king.
The castle once belonged to a powerful abthane.

senses.definition: Plural of abthane: high-ranking thanes or local lords in old Scotland.
examples: The king met with the abthanes to discuss the war.
Several abthanes rebelled against royal taxes.
The chronicle lists the names of the abthanes.

senses.definition: A rare spelling of abstruse, meaning difficult to understand.
examples: The old edition uses the form abtruse instead of abstruse.
He joked about the abtruse language of the contract.
Such abtruse ideas rarely attract a wide audience.

senses.definition: A male given name or short form of a longer name in some cultures; also appears in place names.
examples: Abu moved to London to study engineering.
They visited Abu Simbel during their trip to Egypt.
Abu is a common name in his community.

senses.definition: Full of bubbles or very busy and excited.
examples: The pot was abubble with boiling soup.
The office was abubble with rumors of a merger.
The city was abubble with energy during the festival.

senses.definition: A rare historical or regional term, probably referring to a kind of pipe or musical instrument.
examples: The musician played softly on an abucco.
Old records mention an abucco used in village celebrations.
He collected instruments such as the abucco and the shawm.

senses.definition: In the process of being built.
examples: A new stadium is abuilding on the east side of town.
Several houses are abuilding along the river.
A bridge abuilding here will connect the two districts.

senses.definition: Being built at the present time.
examples: The abuilding stadium dominates the skyline.
Abuilding projects can be seen across the city.
They passed an abuilding tower near the station.
